MobProg.brief: A class representing a mobile program, including its type, arguments, and command list for behavior scripting.
MobProg.details: The MobProg class models mobile programs used within the system to define behaviors and actions for mobile entities. It stores the program's type as a set of bit flags, an argument list for parameterization, and a command list that specifies the behavior scripts. The class provides static methods to convert between human-readable type names and internal bit flag representations, facilitating script parsing and debugging. The constructor initializes a MobProg instance by reading data from a file stream, supporting flexible configuration through external files. Its design includes private copy constructor and assignment operator to prevent unintended copying, ensuring resource safety and object integrity. This class integrates into the larger system architecture by enabling dynamic behavior assignment and management of mobile entities, supporting extensibility and maintainability in behavior scripting and system configuration.
MobProg.notes: The class employs the singleton pattern for static conversion methods and uses private copy constructors and assignment operators to enforce non-copyable semantics, ensuring resource safety. It relies on the Flags::Bit enumeration for efficient type management and string conversion functions for ease of debugging and scripting.
MobProg.rationale: MobProg exists to encapsulate behavior scripts associated with mobile entities, allowing flexible, script-driven actions within the system. By abstracting program types, arguments, and command lists, it supports dynamic behavior configuration, external script loading, and efficient type handling, which are essential for scalable and maintainable game or simulation systems involving mobile characters or objects.
MobProg.name: MobProg

type.brief: Represents the category or attributes of a mobile program using bit flags.
type.details: The `MobProg::type` variable utilizes the `Flags::Bit` enumeration to store multiple category or attribute flags for a mobile program. These flags are typically set during program initialization, often based on input data such as file contents, and are used throughout the codebase to determine behavior, control flow, and validation. By leveraging bitwise operations, this variable enables efficient checking and combination of multiple program types or attributes, supporting flexible and scalable categorization of mobile programs within the system.
type.notes: Since `type` uses bit flags, multiple attributes can be combined and checked simultaneously, which is efficient for permission and feature toggling scenarios.
type.rationale: This variable exists to facilitate quick and flexible categorization of mobile programs, allowing the system to efficiently determine behavior and validation logic based on program type attributes.
type.name: type

usages.class_mobile_prototype, void MobileProto: Checked after creating a new MobProg to determine if it is an invalid program type, leading to an error log and exit.
usages.class_mob_prog, MobProg::MobProg(FILE *f: Assigned the value obtained from 'name_to_type(fread_word(fp))' and used in a switch statement to control subsequent data reading logic.
usages.class_mob_prog, const String MobProg::ty: Passed as an argument to the switch statement to determine which case block to execute based on its value.

arglist.brief: Stores the list of arguments for a mobile program within the MobProg class.
arglist.details: The 'arglist' variable holds a String object that contains the arguments passed to a mobile program. It is typically initialized by reading from a file using fread_string(fp) during the construction of a MobProg instance, provided the program type is valid and not an error. This string encapsulates the parameters that the mobile program will utilize during execution, allowing for flexible and dynamic argument passing. The String class used here extends standard string functionality, offering features such as case-insensitive comparisons and advanced manipulation methods to facilitate argument handling.
arglist.notes: The 'arglist' is stored as a String object, which provides enhanced string manipulation capabilities beyond standard std::string, aiding in argument parsing and processing.
arglist.rationale: This variable exists to enable passing and managing a flexible set of arguments to mobile programs, supporting dynamic behavior based on different input parameters during program execution.
arglist.name: arglist

usages.class_mob_prog, MobProg::MobProg(FILE *f: Assigned the string read from the file by fread_string(fp), representing the argument list for the program, used only if the type is not ERROR_PROG.

comlist.brief: A string that holds the list of commands associated with this MobProg instance.
comlist.details: The `comlist` variable contains a sequence of commands that define the behavior of the MobProg. It is initialized by reading from a file, specifically after the argument list has been processed and discarded, ensuring it only contains relevant command data. The String class used for this variable provides advanced features such as case-insensitive comparisons and manipulation methods, facilitating flexible command parsing and execution within the program's logic. This setup allows the MobProg to interpret and act upon its command list efficiently during runtime.
comlist.notes: The `comlist` is stored as a single string, which may be parsed into individual commands as needed. Its initialization occurs immediately after reading the command data from the file, ensuring it accurately reflects the intended command set for each MobProg instance.
comlist.rationale: This variable exists to store the set of commands that define the behavior of the MobProg, enabling dynamic and flexible behavior scripting. By maintaining the command list as a string, the system can easily parse, modify, and execute commands, supporting complex behavior patterns and customization.
comlist.name: comlist

usages.class_mob_prog, MobProg::MobProg(FILE *f: Assigned the string read from the file by fread_string(fp), representing the command list for the program, read after reading and discarding the argument list.

MobProg::MobProg(FILE *fp).brief: Constructs a MobProg object by reading its type, argument list, and command list from a file stream.
MobProg::MobProg(FILE *fp).details: The constructor begins by reading a word from the provided file stream and converting it into a program type using the name_to_type function. If the resulting type is recognized and not equal to ERROR_PROG, it proceeds to read the argument list string and the command list string from the file, each terminated by a newline. It then advances the file pointer to the start of the next line after each string. This process allows for dynamic loading of mobile program configurations from external files, enabling flexible and data-driven behavior for mobile entities within the system.

params.fp: A pointer to a FILE object from which the mob program data is read.

MobProg::MobProg(FILE *fp).returns: This constructor does not return a value.
MobProg::MobProg(FILE *fp).throws: No exceptions are explicitly thrown; errors in file reading may result in incomplete or invalid object initialization.
MobProg::MobProg(FILE *fp).notes: The constructor handles unrecognized program types by setting the type to ERROR_PROG and skipping the reading of argument and command lists, ensuring robustness against malformed or incomplete data.
MobProg::MobProg(FILE *fp).rationale: This function is used during the loading or initialization phase of mobile programs from external data files, facilitating flexible and configurable behavior for mobile entities based on external scripts or configurations.
MobProg::MobProg(FILE *fp).name: MobProg

virtual MobProg::~MobProg().brief: Virtual destructor for the MobProg class to ensure proper cleanup of derived class objects.
virtual MobProg::~MobProg().details: This destructor is declared as virtual to guarantee that the correct destructor is invoked for objects of derived classes when deleted through a pointer to MobProg. Although the destructor body is empty, declaring it virtual is crucial for proper resource management and avoiding undefined behavior in polymorphic class hierarchies. It allows derived class destructors to execute appropriately, ensuring all resources are released correctly. This design ensures safe and predictable cleanup in inheritance scenarios involving MobProg and its subclasses.
virtual MobProg::~MobProg().returns: This function does not return a value.
virtual MobProg::~MobProg().notes: Having a virtual destructor is essential in polymorphic base classes to prevent resource leaks and undefined behavior during object destruction.
virtual MobProg::~MobProg().rationale: This destructor is used when deleting objects through base class pointers, ensuring that the destructors of derived classes are called correctly, which is critical for proper resource deallocation and avoiding memory leaks.
virtual MobProg::~MobProg().name: ~MobProg

Flags::Bit MobProg::name_to_type(const S.brief: Converts a string identifier of a mob program type into its corresponding bit flag.
Flags::Bit MobProg::name_to_type(const S.details: This function maps a human-readable string representing a specific mob program type to its associated bit flag constant defined in the MobProgTypes enumeration. It performs a series of string comparisons to identify the correct program type and returns the corresponding Flags::Bit value. If the input string does not match any known program type, it returns the ERROR_PROG flag, indicating an unrecognized or invalid program name. This conversion facilitates the interpretation of script files and configuration data by translating textual identifiers into efficient bitwise flags used internally for trigger detection and handling. The function is primarily used during the loading and parsing of mobile program scripts, enabling the system to recognize and activate appropriate behaviors based on textual configuration inputs.

params.name: A string representing the name of the mob program type to be converted.

Flags::Bit MobProg::name_to_type(const S.returns: The Flags::Bit value corresponding to the specified program type, or ERROR_PROG if the name is unrecognized.
Flags::Bit MobProg::name_to_type(const S.notes: The function relies on exact string matches; unrecognized or misspelled names will result in the ERROR_PROG flag. It is case-sensitive based on the current implementation. This method simplifies the process of translating textual script identifiers into internal bit flags used for trigger management.
Flags::Bit MobProg::name_to_type(const S.rationale: This function is used when loading or interpreting mobile program scripts, allowing the system to convert human-readable program type names into internal bit flags for efficient trigger handling and behavior activation.
Flags::Bit MobProg::name_to_type(const S.name: name_to_type

usages.class_mob_prog, MobProg::MobProg(FILE *f: Called with a word read from the file to convert it into a program type bit flag, which then influences the control flow and data reading logic.

const String MobProg::type_to_name(Flags.brief: Converts a mobile program bit flag into its corresponding human-readable name.
const String MobProg::type_to_name(Flags.details: This function takes a Flags::Bit value that represents a specific type of mobile program, such as action, speech, or fight triggers. It employs a switch statement to map each recognized bit flag to a descriptive string identifier, which aids in identifying the program type during debugging, logging, or user interface display. If the input bit flag does not match any known program type, the function returns 'ERROR_PROG' to indicate an unrecognized or invalid value. This mapping ensures consistent and clear representation of program types across various system components, facilitating easier maintenance and understanding of mobile scripting behaviors.

params.type: The Flags::Bit enumeration value indicating the specific mobile program type.

const String MobProg::type_to_name(Flags.returns: A String with the human-readable name of the program type, or 'ERROR_PROG' if the input is unrecognized.
const String MobProg::type_to_name(Flags.notes: The function relies on predefined constants representing different program types, enabling straightforward extension if new types are added. The default case ensures that unrecognized flags are flagged explicitly, aiding in debugging and validation.
const String MobProg::type_to_name(Flags.rationale: This function is used whenever a human-readable representation of a mobile program type is needed, such as in status displays, logs, or debugging tools, to improve clarity and maintainability of scripting and behavior management within the system.
const String MobProg::type_to_name(Flags.name: type_to_name

usages.mob__commands_8cc, void do_mpstat(Charac: Converts a program type flag into its human-readable name for display purposes.

MobProg::MobProg(const MobProg &).brief: Copy constructor for the MobProg class.
MobProg::MobProg(const MobProg &).details: This constructor creates a new instance of the MobProg class by copying the state of an existing MobProg object. It is used to duplicate MobProg objects, ensuring that the new object is an exact copy of the original.
MobProg::MobProg(const MobProg &).name: MobProg

MobProg & MobProg::operator=(const MobPr.brief: Assigns the values from another MobProg object to this one.
MobProg & MobProg::operator=(const MobPr.details: This copy assignment operator allows one MobProg object to be assigned the values of another MobProg object. It ensures that the current object is updated to reflect the state of the source object, effectively making a copy of its data members.

params.rhs: The MobProg object whose values are to be copied.

MobProg & MobProg::operator=(const MobPr.returns: A reference to the current MobProg object after assignment.
MobProg & MobProg::operator=(const MobPr.name: operator=
